title: Course Schedule II
description: There are a total of numCourses courses you have to take, labeled from 0 to numCourses - 1. You are given an array prerequisites where prerequisites[i] = [ai, bi] indicates that you must take course bi first if you want to take course ai. Return the ordering of courses you should take to finish all courses. If there are many valid answers, return any of them. If it is impossible to finish all courses, return an empty array.
constraints: All the pairs [ai, bi] are distinct
thinking_questions: Same as Course Schedule I, but now return the actual ordering. What ordering do prerequisites define?
Topological sort gives a valid ordering. How do you build one from DFS?
When does a node get added to the order?

hints.L1: Topological sort: same DFS as Course Schedule I, but append each node to result AFTER processing all its prerequisites.
hints.L2: Post-order DFS: recurse on all prerequisites, then add current node to result. Reverse at the end (or use prepend).
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: topological sort = postorder DFS on DAG
adj = defaultdict(list)
for a, b in prerequisites: adj[b].append(a)
state = [0] * numCourses; result = []
def dfs(node):
    if state[node] == 1: return False  # cycle
    if state[node] == 2: return True
    state[node] = 1
    for neighbor in adj[node]:
        if not dfs(neighbor): return False
    state[node] = 2
    result.append(node)  # add after all deps processed
    return True
for i in range(numCourses):
    if not dfs(i): return []
return result[::-1]
# Time: O(V+E)  Space: O(V+E)

follow_up_questions: Why does postorder (add after recursing) give a valid topological order?
Why do you need to reverse the result at the end?
How does Kahn's BFS algorithm find topological order? Compare to DFS.
solution_notes: Postorder DFS: add node after processing all deps. Reverse result. Return [] on cycle. O(V+E).
